title: Variable Acceleration — Calculus in Kinematics
description: Motion where acceleration is not constant: using differentiation to move from displacement to velocity to acceleration, and integration to reverse the process, finding constants of integration from initial conditions.
objectives: Differentiate displacement with respect to time to find velocity, and velocity to find acceleration
Integrate acceleration to find velocity, and velocity to find displacement, using initial conditions to determine constants of integration
Recognise when the SUVAT equations do not apply and calculus is required instead
Find times and positions of zero velocity, and use calculus to locate maximum or minimum displacement and velocity
Apply these techniques to solve multi-part kinematics problems involving polynomial functions of time

lessons.title: Differentiating motion: from displacement to acceleration
Integrating motion: from acceleration back to displacement
Applications: turning points, extremes and multi-part problems
